source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure working of EDR

input_row.source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure working of EDR
input_row.audit: 1. Preparing the Environment for EDR - Before getting started with EDR, you must prepare the environment that you want to back up. 2. Preparing the Source Server - Allow direct access to Elastic Disaster Recovery and Amazon S3 AWS service API endpoints through HTTPS protocol (TCP port 443). Direct outbound TCP port 1500 from the source server to the staging area subnet, which contains the replication servers. 3. Preparing the Staging Area Subnet - Allow Direct access to EDR, S3, and EC2 through HTTPS protocol (TCP port 443) Direct inbound TCP port 1500 for replication traffic 4. Accessing the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery Console - o Search for вЂњAWS Elastic Disaster RecoveryвЂќ in the AWS Console. o Select вЂњElastic Disaster RecoveryвЂќ 5. Configuring the Replication Settings Template - Select Configure and Initializein in the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery screen. You will be navigated to setup your replication settings template. This will create a staging area in a subnet of your choice and a replication server instance types. The default replication server instance type will be a t3 micro EC2 instance. This is good for normal workloads with small I/O operations. 6. Next, configure EBS encryption and volume types. This will depend on your workload requirements. 7. To encrypt EBS volumes, leave the setting as вЂњdefault.вЂќ If you wish to make a custom encryption setting, you will need to create an AWS KMS key. 8. Configure the security group to your specific needs. Remember what ports need to be opened on inbound / outbound traffic that was specified in previous steps: You can choose how you want your data routed and if you want to throttle network traffic to reserve bandwidth. To keep your data as secure as possible, itвЂ™s recommended to get set up with a VPN or AWS direct connect, so your backups are not traveling over the public internet. Point in time policy defines the snapshot retention time. Because Elastic Disaster Recovery service uses incremental backups, itвЂ™s not necessary to keep old copies of backups. Now, youвЂ™re ready to launch this template.

input_row.justification: While certain aspects of AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (EDR) setupвЂ”such as security group rules, EBS encryption, and subnet configurationsвЂ”can be programmatically checked via AWS APIs, the core of this control (6.13) is to ensure the "working of EDR." This requires validating the end-to-end operational readiness, including environment preparation, network connectivity, and actual backup/replication functionality, which involves steps outside the scope of what AWS APIs can fully verify. Critical elements, such as confirming direct access from source servers, validating outbound port access, and ensuring the EDR process is functioning as intended, require manual inspection, test failovers, and human judgment.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Access the AWS Management Console and navigate to the "AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery" service. 2. Confirm that all source servers intended for protection are listed and in a "Healthy" or "Protected" state. 3. For each source server: - Verify that outbound HTTPS (TCP port 443) access to AWS EDR and S3 endpoints is allowed (check security group and NACL rules). - Confirm that outbound TCP port 1500 to the staging area subnet is permitted from the source server. - Ensure the staging area subnet has direct access to EDR, S3, and EC2 services. 4. Review the replication settings and ensure that replication is active and up-to-date for each protected server. 5. Perform or review a recent test failover or drill to confirm that EDR is functioning as intended (i.e., recovery points are usable and servers can be restored). 6. Document all findings, including screenshots or logs of configuration and test results.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
The audit control "Ensure working of EDR" involves validating the end-to-end operational readiness of AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (EDR). While some aspects such as security groups and replication settings can be checked programmatically, confirming that EDR is functioning correctly requires manual validation, as it involves testing environments, verifying network connectivity, and assessing operational processes.

**MANUAL STEPS:**

1. **Access the AWS Management Console** and navigate to the **"AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery"** service.
2. **Check the status of source servers**: Confirm that all source servers intended for protection are listed and in a **"Healthy"** or **"Protected"** state.
3. For each source server:
   - **Inspect Security Group settings**: Verify that outbound HTTPS (TCP port 443) access to AWS EDR and S3 endpoints is allowed.
   - **Confirm outbound rules**: Ensure that outbound TCP port 1500 to the staging area subnet is permitted from the source server.
4. **Review the staging area subnet**: Ensure it has direct access to EDR, S3, and EC2 services.
5. **Check replication settings**: Review the replication settings for each protected server to ensure replication is active and up-to-date.
6. **Perform or review a recent test failover or drill**: Confirm that EDR is functioning as intended (i.e., that recovery points are usable and servers can be restored).
7. **Document all findings**: Include screenshots or logs of configuration and test results for compliance records. 

These manual steps will help ensure the EDR setup is not only correctly configured but also actively working as intended for disaster recovery operations.
